label: faithful
notes: A high-stakes medical-adjacent question where the panel splits on a polar marker (two models recommend, two say avoid). The synthesis keeps the split visible and carries a high-stakes notice. The label asserts the ENGINE's structural verdicts (grounded markers, disagreement preserved, warning present) - it is NOT a clinical judgement about which side is right; see needs_operator in the README.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Reading of the trial evidence

On the randomised evidence available, I would recommend time-restricted eating as one option within a structured programme for adults with type 2 diabetes, provided medication is reviewed first. The pooled trials show glycaemic improvement broadly comparable to continuous energy restriction, with better adherence in some arms [1].

The qualifier matters more than the headline: the trials enrolled adults without advanced kidney disease, and hypoglycaemia risk rises sharply for anyone on sulfonylureas or insulin unless dosing is adjusted in advance [2]. That is a prescriber decision, not a lifestyle one.

I would not present this as a settled first-line intervention. It is one arm of a programme, and the strength of the evidence is moderate rather than strong.

sources.title: Randomised trials of time-restricted eating in type 2 diabetes
ADA Standards of Care: Nutrition Therapy

initial_answers.answer_text:
### Why I land the other way

I would avoid presenting fasting protocols as a management option for type 2 diabetes outside a supervised setting. The same trial set that shows glycaemic benefit also reports a materially higher rate of symptomatic hypoglycaemia in participants on insulin or sulfonylureas, and the dose adjustments that prevent it were made by clinicians inside the protocol [2].

Two further limits on the evidence base:

- Follow-up in most arms is 12 weeks or less, so durability is unestablished [1].
- Drop-out was higher in the fasting arms of the larger trials, which flatters the completer analysis.

> The honest summary is that this is a supervised intervention with a real adverse-event profile, not a general dietary suggestion.

Anyone weighing it needs their prescriber in the conversation before any change.

sources.title: Randomised trials of time-restricted eating in type 2 diabetes
ADA Standards of Care: Nutrition Therapy

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Position

I would recommend it conditionally. Where the person is managed on metformin alone and has no history of disordered eating, the balance of the randomised evidence favours offering time-restricted eating alongside standard nutrition therapy [1], and the standards-of-care text treats structured energy restriction as an acceptable route to remission-oriented weight loss [2].

The conditions are doing real work in that sentence:

- Medication review first, because the hypoglycaemia signal is concentrated in insulin and sulfonylurea users [2].
- A plan for what happens on days the protocol is missed, which is where adherence data degrades.

See [the ADA nutrition standards](https://diabetesjournals.org/care/article/47/Supplement_1/S77) for the framing this follows. The residual uncertainty is durability beyond one year, which no trial in this set answers.

sources.title: Randomised trials of time-restricted eating in type 2 diabetes
ADA Standards of Care: Nutrition Therapy

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Where I come out.** I would avoid it as a default suggestion, for a reason that is about the population rather than the physiology.

**The argument.** The trials that show benefit recruited motivated volunteers under supervision, screened out advanced complications, and adjusted medication before the intervention started [1]. Outside that frame the same protocol lands on people whose regimen is not adjusted, and the adverse-event profile is dominated by hypoglycaemia in insulin and sulfonylurea users [2].

**What would change my position.** A pragmatic trial in unselected primary-care populations with 12-month follow-up. Nothing in the current set is that trial.

**Limits.** I am reading trial summaries, not individual patient data, and this is not a substitute for a clinician who knows the person's regimen.

sources.title: Randomised trials of time-restricted eating in type 2 diabetes
ADA Standards of Care: Nutrition Therapy

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees on the mechanism and on the population in which harm concentrates: benefit is plausible and trial-supported for people managed without insulin or sulfonylureas, and the hypoglycaemia risk for those medications is the dominant adverse-event signal [1] [2].
final_synthesis.disagreement: The panel is split two against two on the framing, and the split is material rather than semantic. Two models would offer time-restricted eating as a conditional option inside a structured programme; two would withhold it as a default suggestion outside supervision, on the grounds that the trials screened and medicated their populations first. Neither side disputes the other's evidence - they weight the unselected-population risk differently.
final_synthesis.source_support: Both positions rest on the same two sources: the randomised trial synthesis and [the ADA nutrition standards](https://diabetesjournals.org/care/article/47/Supplement_1/S77). No model introduced evidence the others did not see.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: Durability beyond twelve months is unestablished in every trial cited, drop-out was higher in the fasting arms, and no trial in this set enrolled an unselected primary-care population.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Treat this as decision support for a conversation with a prescriber, not as a recommendation. The medication review is the step both sides agree must come first.
final_synthesis.high_stakes_notice: This question is health-related. The panel provides decision support only; it is not medical advice, and no output here should change a treatment or medication without a qualified clinician.
